blocks.text: # Why do we need a Constitution?

items: The first function of a constitution is to provide **a set of basic rules** that allow for minimal coordination amongst members of a society
The second function of a constitution is to specify **who has the power** to make decisions in a society. It decides how the government will be constituted.
The third function of a constitution is to **set some limits on what a government can impose on its citizens.** These limits are fundamental in the sense that government may never trespass them
The fourth function of a constitution **is to enable the government to fulfil the aspirations of a society** and create conditions for a just society.

blocks.text: The Constitution of India is the supreme law that lays down the framework for governance, political principles, and citizens' rights.
# Salient Features of the Indian Constitution

items: 1) Lengthiest Written Constitution
2) Drawn from Various Sources
3) Blend of Rigidity and Flexibility
4) Federal System with Unitary Bias
5) Parliamentary Form of Government
6) Synthesis of Parliamentary Sovereignty and Judicial Supremacy
7) Integrated and Independent Judiciary
8) Fundamental Rights (Part III)
9) Directive Principles of State Policy (Part IV)
10) Fundamental Duties (Part IV-A)
11) Secular State
12) Universal Adult Franchise
13) Single Citizenship
14) Independent Bodies (e.g., ECI, CAG, UPSC)
15) Emergency Provisions
16) Three-Tier Government (Panchayats and Municipalities)

rows: Shortest written constitution (Originally 7 Articles). Lays down only the framework and basic principles.
Rigid (Difficult to amend). Details added by conventions and court rulings.
Not a single document. Composed of statutes, conventions, and judicial precedents.
Flexible (Can be amended by ordinary law).
Longest written constitution (Originally 395 Articles, now approx. 470). Highly detailed and elaborate.

blocks.text:
Rigid Constitution
• Requires special procedure for amendment.
• Example: American Constitution.

Flexible Constitution
• Amended like ordinary laws.
• Example: British Constitution.

Indian Constitution → Synthesis of Both
Neither fully rigid nor fully flexible.

1. Simple Majority of Parliament
• Certain provisions amended like ordinary legislation.
• These do not fall under Article 368.
# Article 368 provides for two types of amendments :

1. Special Majority of Parliament
• Two-thirds majority of members present & voting.
• Majority of total membership of each House.
2. Special Majority + Ratification by States
• Requires Special Majority and ratification by half of the total states

# Features of Parliamentary Government in India

items: 1) Presence of nominal executive (President/Governor) & real executive (PM/CM).

blocks.text:
• India: Parliament is not sovereign; bound by Constitution.
• India: Head of State is elected (Republic).

• Britain: Parliament is sovereign;
• Head of State is hereditary monarch
India blends British parliamentary sovereignty with American judicial supremacy.

items: Parliament → can amend most of the Constitution.
Supreme Court → can strike down unconstitutional laws through judicial review.
Balance ensured: neither Parliament nor Judiciary is absolutely supreme, but Constitution remains supreme.
Single hierarchy of courts: Supreme Court → High Courts → Subordinate Courts (District & lower courts).
Enforces both Union and State laws.
Contrast: USA has dual judiciary (separate federal & state courts).

blocks.text: Judiciary in India is integrated (single system) and independent (protected from interference) → ensures constitutional supremacy and protection of citizens' rights.

blocks.text: A preamble is the **introductory statement** that sets out the **philosophy, values, and objectives** of the Constitution.

items: First Use:
The American Constitution was the first to begin with a Preamble.
Jurist's View:
N.A. Palkhivala called the Preamble the "identity card of the Constitution."
K.M. Munshi called the Preamble the "Horoscope of the Constitution"
Indian Context:
Based on the Objectives Resolution (1946), drafted and moved by Pandit Jawaharlal Nehru.
Adopted by the Constituent Assembly in 1947.
Amendment:
The 42nd Constitutional Amendment Act (1976) amended the Preamble.
Added three new words: Socialist, Secular, Integrity.

blocks.text: Let us read the Preamble of our Constitution very carefully and understand the meaning of each of its key words.

The Preamble of the Constitution reads like a poem on democracy. It contains the philosophy on which the entire Constitution has been built. It provides a standard to examine and evaluate any law and action of government, to find out whether it is good or bad. It is the soul of the Indian Constitution.

**WE, THE PEOPLE OF INDIA**, having solemnly resolved to constitute India into a **SOVEREIGN**, **SOCIALIST**, **SECULAR**, **DEMOCRATIC REPUBLIC** and to secure to all its citizens: **JUSTICE**, social, economic and political; **LIBERTY** of thought, expression, belief, faith and worship; **EQUALITY** of status and of opportunity; and to pomote among them all **FRATERNITY** assuring the dignity of the individual and the unity and integrity of the Nation; IN OUR CONSTITUENT ASSEMBLY this twenty-sixth day of November, 1949, do HEREBY ADOPT, ENACT AND GIVE TO OURSELVES THIS CONSTITUTION.
WE, THE PEOPLE OF INDIA — The constitution has been drawn up and enacted by the people through their representatives, and not handed down to them by a king or any outside powers.
SOVEREIGN — People have supreme right to make decisions on internal as well as external matters. No external power can dictate the government of India.
SOCIALIST — Wealth is generated socially and should be shared equally by society. Government should regulate the ownership of land and industry to reduce socio-economic inequalities.
SECULAR — Citizens have complete freedom to follow any religion. But there is no official religion. Government treats all religious beliefs and practices with equal respect.
DEMOCRATIC — A form of government where people enjoy equal political rights, elect their rulers and hold them accountable. The government is run according to some basic rules.
REPUBLIC — The head of the state is an elected person and not a hereditary position.

JUSTICE — Citizens cannot be discriminated on the grounds of caste, religion and gender. Social inequalities have to be reduced. Government should work for the welfare of all, especially of the disadvantaged groups.
LIBERTY — There are no unreasonable restrictions on the citizens in what they think, how they wish to express their thoughts and the way they wish to follow up their thoughts in action.
EQUALITY — All are equal before the law. The traditional social inequalities have to be ended. The government should ensure equal opportunity for all.
FRATERNITY — All of us should behave as if we are members of the same family. No one should treat a fellow citizen as inferior.

WE, THE PEOPLE OF INDIA (Source of Authority), having solemnly resolved to constitute India into a SOVEREIGN **SOCIALIST SECULAR** DEMOCRATIC REPUBLIC (SSSDR) (Nature), and to secure to all its citizens:
LIBERTY(L) of thought, expression, belief, faith and worship (TEBFAW);
EQUALITY(E) of status and of opportunity (SO);
and to promote among them all
FRATERNITY(F) assuring the dignity of the individual and the unity and **integrity** of the Nation (DUI); (JLEF = Objective),
IN OUR CONSTITUENT ASSEMBLY this twenty-sixth day of November, 1949 (Date of Adoption), do HEREBY ADOPT, ENACT AND GIVE TO OURSELVES (AEG) THIS CONSTITUTION.
# 42nd CAA Added three new words : Socialist, Secular, Integrity (SIS).

Implies: No privileged class; all public offices open to citizens equally.

Economic justice → eliminate inequalities in wealth, income, property; distributive justice.
Political justice → equal political rights, equal access to offices, equal voice in government.
Source: Idea taken from Russian Revolution (1917).

items: Citizenship provisions are in Part II (Articles 5-11) of the Constitution.
India follows the system of single citizenship (like Britain).
Article 5: Citizenship at the commencement of the Constitution - persons domiciled in India with birth, parentage, or residence qualifications.
Article 6: Rights of citizenship for migrants from Pakistan.
Article 7: Rights of citizenship for migrants to Pakistan but later returning to India.
Article 8: Citizenship for persons of Indian origin residing outside India.
Article 9: No dual citizenship - persons voluntarily acquiring foreign citizenship cannot be Indian citizens.
Article 10: Continuance of rights of citizenship subject to law made by Parliament.
Article 11: Parliament empowered to regulate citizenship by law.

blocks.text: A. Five Ways of Acquiring Citizenship

items.text: By Birth - (After Dec 3, 2004: child born in India is a citizen only if both parents are citizens, or one is a citizen and the other is not an illegal migrant).
By Descent - (Born outside India: at least one parent must be a citizen, and registration within 1 year is required).
By Registration - (Persons of Indian origin, spouses of Indian citizens, etc., after 7 years of residence).
By Naturalisation - (After 12 years of residence or service in India).
By Incorporation of Territory - (If a foreign territory becomes part of India, e.g., Goa, Puducherry).

pages.topic: Part I and Part II of The Constitution

blocks.text: B. Three Ways of Losing Citizenship

items.text: By Renunciation - Voluntary declaration to give up citizenship.
By Termination - Automatically ends if a person voluntarily acquires citizenship of another country.
By Deprivation - Government can withdraw citizenship if obtained fraudulently, disloyalty to the Constitution, or continuous residence abroad for 7 years.

blocks.text:
• Article 13(1): Pre-Constitution laws inconsistent with Fundamental Rights → void to the extent of inconsistency (Doctrine of Eclipse).
• Article 13(2): Post-Constitution laws violating Fundamental Rights → void ab initio (Doctrine of Severability applies to strike down offending parts).

• Eclipse = temporary shadow (pre-Constitution laws, can revive)
• Severability = cut off bad part (post-Constitution laws, rest survives)

• Article 13(3):
  Law Includes: This term is wide, covering Parliamentary Acts, Ordinances, Delegated Legislation (rules/orders), and Customs.
• Article 13(4): Added in 24th CAA (To protect amendment from JR)

items: The State shall not deny to any person equality before the law or the equal protection of the laws within the territory of India.

blocks.text:
Equality before law - British, negative (no privileges, none above law).
Equal protection of laws - American, positive (like treated alike).

Rule of Law (Dicey):
1. No arbitrary power (Supremacy of Law) - punishment only for breach of law.
2. Equality Before the Law- all persons under ordinary courts.
3. Predominance of Legal Spirit (Source of Rights) - not applicable in India (here Constitution is source of rights).
• SC View: Rule of Law under Art. 14 = basic feature → cannot be destroyed even by amendment.

rows: Freedom of **S**peech and Expression (Includes Freedom of the Press, Right to Know, Right to Silence).

items: Originally 7 rights; property right deleted by 44th Amendment (1978).
Rights available only to citizens (not foreigners/companies).
Protected against State action only, not private individuals.
Restrictions must be reasonable and only on grounds specified in Article 19.

items.text: Speech & Expression – includes press freedom, ads, telecast, right to know, silence, demonstration (not strike).
items.note: (i) Restrictions: sovereignty & integrity, security of state, foreign relations, public order, decency/morality, contempt of court, defamation, incitement.

items.text: Assembly – peaceful, unarmed meetings/processions.
items.note: (i) Restrictions: sovereignty & integrity, public order. (ii) IPC 141 → unlawful assembly (≥5 persons with illegal object). (iii) CrPC 144 → magistrate can restrain assembly.

items.text: Association/Union/Co-op Societies – includes political parties, trade unions, clubs, etc.
items.note: (i) Restrictions: sovereignty & integrity, public order, morality. (ii) No fundamental right to recognition; no guaranteed right to strike/lockout.

items.text: Movement – free movement across India (internal).
items.note: (i) Restrictions: public interest, protection of Scheduled Tribes. (ii) SC: prostitutes' movement restricted (public health/morals); (iii) HC: AIDS restrictions valid.External movement (exit/entry) covered under Article 21.

items.text: Residence & Settlement – reside temporarily or settle permanently anywhere.
items.note: (i) Restrictions: public interest, protection Scheduled Tribes. (ii) SC: certain areas can be banned for habitual offenders/prostitutes. (iii) Overlaps with freedom of movement.

items.text: Profession/Occupation/Trade/Business – wide scope, covers livelihood.
items.note: (i) Restrictions: public interest. (ii) State can prescribe qualifications, regulate/prohibit immoral/dangerous trades, and run monopolies.

blocks.text: Article 20 protects accused persons (all persons) with three safeguards:

items: No Ex-Post-Facto Law (Art. 20(1)): ↳ No Retrospective Criminal Law (Conviction/Penalty). ↳ Only applies to Criminal Law (not Civil/Tax).
No Double Jeopardy (Art. 20(2)): ↳ No Prosecution/Punishment for the Same Offence Twice. ↳ Applies Only in Courts/Judicial Tribunals (not departmental).
No Self-Incrimination (Art. 20(3)): ↳ No Compulsion to be a Witness Against Oneself. ↳ Includes Oral/Documentary Evidence. ↳ Excludes: Material Objects, Signatures, Thumb Impressions.

blocks.text: Protection of Life and Personal Liberty (Citizens & Non- Citizens)
No person shall be deprived of his life or personal liberty except according to procedure established by law.

rows: Procedure AND Fairness/Justice of the law.

items: A.K. Gopalan Case (1950) → Narrow view; protection only against executive action, not legislative

blocks.text: A.K. Gopalan Case (1950) ⇒ Maneka Gandhi Case (1978) (a large downward arrow on the page connects the two, showing the progression)

items: Maneka Gandhi Case (1978) → Wider view; procedure must be fair, just, reasonable, protection against executive + legislative action, "personal liberty" = widest amplitude.
Expanded meaning: Right to life = not mere survival, but dignified existence.

blocks.text:
1980s-1990s → Rights added: livelihood, shelter, health, speedy trial, education, environment. etc.

Right to free and compulsory education

items: State shall provide free and compulsory education to all children aged 6-14 years.
Covers elementary education only, not higher/professional education.

After 86th CAA (2002) amendment: ↳ Article 45 revised → early childhood care & education up to 6 years. ↳ New Fundamental Duty (Art. 51A) → parents must provide education opportunities to children 6-14 years.

blocks.text: Protection in Cases of Arrest and Detention
I. Punitive Detention → punishment after trial & conviction.
II. Preventive Detention → detention without trial, to prevent future offence; precautionary, based on suspicion.

items: Right to be informed of grounds of arrest.
Right to consult & be defended by a lawyer.
Right to be produced before magistrate within 24 hours (excluding travel time).
Right to release after 24 hours unless magistrate authorises further detention.
Not applicable to enemy aliens or preventive detention cases.
SC clarified: does not cover arrest with court orders , civil arrest, tax default, deportation.
Applies to citizens + aliens.
Max detention = 3 months, unless Advisory Board (HC judges) approves extension.
Grounds of detention must be communicated (except matters against public interest).
Detenu can make representation against detention.
Parliament powers: ↳ Can allow detention >3 months without Advisory Board opinion. ↳ Can fix max detention period for different cases. ↳ Can prescribe Advisory Board procedure.
44th Amendment (1978) → reduced period to 2 months, but not enforced.

blocks.text:
Legislative Powers
Parliament exclusive → defence, foreign affairs, security of India.
Parliament + States concurrent → state security, public order, essential supplies/services.

items: Prohibits human trafficking, begar (forced labour), bonded labour; applies to citizens & non-citizens; protects against State and private persons.
Exception: compulsory service for public purposes (e.g., military/social service) allowed, but no discrimination.
Important laws: Immoral Traffic (Prevention) Act, 1956; Bonded Labour Abolition Act, 1976; Minimum Wages Act, 1948.
Prohibits employment of children below 14 years in factories, mines, or hazardous work.

Renamed principal law as Child & Adolescent Labour (Prohibition & Regulation) Act, 1986.

Adolescents (14-18 yrs) → prohibited from hazardous occupations.

Guarantees: freedom of conscience + right to profess, practice, propagate religion.
Subject to public order, morality, health, other fundamental rights.

Explanations: ↳ Kirpans included in Sikh profession. ↳ Hindus include Sikhs, Jains, Buddhists.
Note: Propagation ≠ forcible conversion.
Rights of religious denominations: ↳ Establish & maintain institutions. ↳ Manage religious affairs. ↳ Own & acquire property. ↳ Administer property as per law.
Subject to public order, morality, health.

Examples: Ramakrishna Mission, Ananda Marga = denominations; Aurobindo Society ≠ denomination.
No person can be compelled to pay taxes for promotion/ maintenance of a particular religion.
State cannot favour one religion over another.
Tax vs Fee: Tax prohibited; fee allowed for secular regulation (e.g., pilgrim services, endowment management).
Institutions wholly maintained by State → religious instruction prohibited.
Institutions under endowment/trust but administered by State → instruction permitted.
Recognised/State-aided institutions → instruction voluntary (consent required, guardian's consent for minors).

items: Part IV, Articles 36-51 of the Constitution
Borrowed : From Irish Constitution (1937), which itself drew from the Spanish Constitution

blocks.text: # Features of the Directive Principles of State Policy (DPSP)

items: Guiding Ideals – Instructions for governance
Defnition of State (Art. 36) = Centre, States, local authorities. (Same as in Part III)
Historical Link – Like Instrument of Instructions (1935)
Ambedkar : "instructions" to legislature/executive.
Socio-economic agenda,
Aims Justice, Liberty, Equality, Fraternity (Preamble)
builds Welfare State not Police State
Non-Justiciable – Not enforceable,
Article 37: Still fundamental to governance ; ↳ Duty of State to apply.
Judicial Utility – Courts uphold laws advancing DPSPs; ↳ Saved as reasonable in relation to Articles 14 & 19
Helps courts in determining the constitutionality of any law

Defines the "State" in Part IV as having the same meaning as in Part III (Fundamental Rights), which includes the Government and Parliament of India, the Government and Legislature of each of the States, and all local or other authorities within the territory of India or under the control of the Government of India.

States that the provisions contained in Part IV shall not be enforceable by any court, but the principles therein laid down are nevertheless fundamental in the governance of the country and it shall be the duty of the State to apply these principles in making laws.

Real Sanction (Force): It is political-rooted in Public Opinion and the electorate. Governments must answer to the voters (Dr. Ambedkar) if they ignore them.

items: 1. Socialistic Principles

These principles aim at promoting the welfare of the people by securing a social order permeated by social, economic, and political justice.

These principles embody the ideology of liberalism, focusing on a framework of individual liberty and rational administration.

blocks.text: # Criticism of the Directive Principles of State Policy

items.text: K.T. Shah: "Pious superfluities" - like a cheque payable only when resources permit.
Nasiruddin: Compared them to New Year's resolutions - easily broken.
T.T. Krishnamachari: "Veritable dustbin of sentiments."
K.C. Wheare: "Manifesto of aims and aspirations".
Sir Ivor Jennings: Only pious aspirations.

items: Article 37 : Declared by the Constitution as fundamental to governance.
L.M. Singhvi: "Life-giving provisions" - embody philosophy of social justice.
M.C. Chagla: If fully implemented, India would be a welfare state and "heaven on earth."
Dr. B.R. Ambedkar: Value lies in establishing economic democracy (beyond political democracy).
Granville Austin: Aim to further social revolution and create conditions for its achievement.
Sir B.N. Rau: Serve as moral precepts with educative value.

blocks.text: # Conflicts between Fundamental Rights & Directive Principles

items: Fundamental Rights (FRs): Justiciable, legally enforceable.
Directive Principles (DPs): Non-justiciable, only moral obligation (Art. 37).
Leads to clash between enforceable rights and guiding principles.

blocks.text: # Article 39 - Certain Principles of Policy to be followed by the State
(b): The State shall direct its policy towards ensuring that the **ownership and control** of the material resources of the community are distributed in such a way as to best serve the common good.
(c): The State shall ensure that the operation of the economic system does not result in the **concentration of wealth** and means of production to the common detriment.

items: FRs prevail over DPs.
DPs are subsidiary to FRs.
Parliament can amend FRs to implement DPs → led to 1st (1951), 4th (1955), 17th (1964) Amendments.
FRs are sacrosanct → cannot be amended to implement DPs.
Parliament's Response:
↳ 24th Amendment (1971): Parliament can amend FRs.
↳ 25th Amendment (1971): Inserted Article 31C:
↳ ↳ Laws implementing Art. 39(b) & (c) not void even if violating Art. 14, 19, 31.
↳ ↳ Such laws cannot be questioned in courts.
Struck down 2nd provision of Art. 31C (removing judicial review).
Upheld 1st provision (priority of Art. 39(b), (c) over Art. 14, 19, 31).
Extended Art. 31C protection to all DPs.
Gave primacy to DPs over FRs (Art. 14, 19, 31).
Struck down 42nd Amendment's extension.
Restored balance: FRs generally supreme, but Art. 39(b), (c) can override Art. 14 & 19.
Famous dictum:
↳ FRs & DPs are "two wheels of a chariot"
↳ Harmony is part of basic structure.
44th Amendment (1978): Abolished Right to Property (Art. 31) as FR.

blocks.text: It shall be the duty of every citizen of India-

items: (a) to abide by the Constitution and respect its ideals and institutions, the National Flag and the National Anthem;
(b) to cherish and follow the noble ideals which inspired our national struggle for freedom;
(c) to uphold and protect the sovereignty, unity and integrity of India;
(d) to defend the country and render national service when called upon to do so;

(e) to promote harmony and the spirit of brotherhood amongst all the people of India transcending religious, linguistic and regional diversities; to renounce practices derogatory to the dignity of women;
(f) to value and preserve the rich heritage of our composite culture;
(g) to protect and improve the natural environment including forests, lakes, rivers and wildlife, and to show compassion for living creatures;
(h) to develop scientific temper, humanism and the spirit of inquiry and reform;
(i) to safeguard public property and to abjure violence;
(j) to strive towards excellence in all spheres of individual and collective activity so that the nation rises to higher levels of achievement;
(k) who is a parent or guardian to provide education to children (6-14 years) - added by the 86th Amendment Act, 2002.

blocks.text: # Verma Committee Observations (1999) - Legal Provisions for Fundamental Duties

items: 1. Prevention of Insults to National Honour Act (1971): Prevents disrespect to Constitution, Flag, Anthem.
2. Criminal Laws: Punish acts encouraging enmity on grounds of language, race, religion, place of birth.
3. Protection of Civil Rights Act (1955): Punishes offences related to caste & religion.
4. Indian Penal Code (IPC): Declares imputations/acts prejudicial to national integration as punishable.
5. Unlawful Activities (Prevention) Act (1967): Allows declaring communal organisations unlawful.
6. Representation of People Act (1951): Disqualifies MPs/MLAs for corrupt practices (soliciting votes on religion, promoting enmity).
7. Wildlife Protection Act (1972): Prohibits trade in rare & endangered species.
8. Forest Conservation Act (1980): Checks deforestation & diversion of forest land.

blocks.text: # Article 111 : Veto Power of the President
A bill becomes law only after President's assent.
# Options when a bill is presented :

items: Assent → Bill becomes law.
Withhold assent → Bill fails (Absolute Veto)
Return Bill (not Money Bill) → Parliament reconsiders. If re-passed, President must assent (Suspensive Veto)
No Action (Pocket Veto)

blocks.text: Note : No Veto over Constitutional Amendment Bills (24th Amendment, 1971 → assent compulsory)

Absolute Veto Example Example:
• 1954 – Dr. Rajendra Prasad withheld assent to PEPSU Appropriation Bill.
• 1991 – R. Venkataraman withheld assent to Salary, Allowances & Pension of MPs Bill.

Pocket Veto:
• 1986 – Zail Singh kept Indian Post Office (Amendment) Bill pending (restrictions on press).
When a state bill is presented to the Governor, he may:

items: 1. Assent → Bill becomes law.
2. Withhold assent → Bill fails.
3. Return (not Money Bill) → Legislature reconsiders.
4. Reserve for President's consideration.

blocks.text: If a bill is reserved for the President, he may:

items: 1. Assent → Bill becomes law.
2. Withhold assent → Bill fails.
3. Direct Governor to return (not Money Bill) → Legislature reconsiders. ↳ I. Even if re-passed, President is not bound to assent. ↳ II. Hence, state legislature cannot override President's veto.
Nature: Temporary laws, same force as Acts; issued only when Parliament not in session.
Control: Issued/withdrawn only on advice of Council of Ministers.

Limitations: ↳ Valid only when Houses not in session. ↳ Scope same as Parliament; cannot violate Fundamental Rights. ↳ Must be laid before Parliament; lapses after 6 weeks of reassembly unless approved.
Duration: Max life = 6 months + 6 weeks.
Features: Can be retrospective, repeal/amend laws, alter tax laws; cannot amend Constitution.

blocks.text: # Article 72 : Pardoning Power of the President

items: Applies to: ↳ Offences against Union law. ↳ Sentences by court martial. ↳ Death sentences.
Independent of judiciary; exercised on Union Cabinet's advice.
Purpose: correct judicial errors, give relief from harsh sentences.
1. Pardon – Removes conviction & sentence completely.
2. Commutation – Substitutes lighter punishment (e.g., death → life imprisonment).
3. Remission – Reduces period of sentence, nature unchanged.
4. Respite – Lesser sentence due to special circumstances (e.g., disability, pregnancy).
5. Reprieve – Temporary stay of execution (especially death sentence).
1. Pardon – Perfect
2. Commutation – Change to lighter (mutate in lighter version)
3. Remission – Reduced Mission, but mission Unchanged.
4. Respite – Lesser sentence in Special Circumstances
5. Reprieve – Priveledge of Stay of execution but temporary

blocks.text:
Collective Responsibility (Article 75)
• Council jointly responsible to Lok Sabha.
• No-confidence → all resign. (Sail and Sink Together)
• Cabinet decisions bind all; dissenting minister must resign

Individual Responsibility (Article 75)
• Ministers hold office during President's pleasure.
• In practice → dismissal only on PM's advice.
• PM ensures collective responsibility by controlling ministers.

No Legal Responsibility
• Britain: King's orders countersigned → minister legally liable.
• India: No such system; courts barred from probing ministerial advice

items.text: First Reading – Introduction (either Lok Sabha or Rajya Sabha ) + publication in Gazette. No debate.

Second Reading – Most important stage:
I. General discussion → House may: take up, refer to select/joint committee, or circulate for public opinion.
II. Committee stage → clause-by-clause scrutiny, amendments allowed.
III. Consideration stage → House debates clauses, votes, accepts amendments.
Third Reading – Debate only on acceptance/rejection of bill as a whole. If passed → sent to other House.
Second House – Same stages; may pass, amend, reject, or keep pending.
items.note: Deadlock (rejection, non-action for 6 months, disagreement) → President may summon joint sitting.

items.text: President's Assent – May assent, withhold, or return (suspensive veto). If returned and passed again → must assent.

blocks.text: A Parliamentary Committee is a panel of Members of Parliament (MPs), elected or nominated, that works under the direction of the Speaker (Lok Sabha) or Chairman (Rajya Sabha ).

items: Origin: Borrowed from the British parliamentary system.
Authority: Draws power from Article 105 (privileges of MPs and committees) and Article 118 (Parliament's authority to make rules).

blocks.text: Key Role: Scrutiny of bills, budgets, and ministry administration → "Watchdogs of Parliament."

rows: Temporary; created for a specific task.

Functions:
↳ Consider Demands for Grants (post-Budget discussion).

Part XI : Relations between the Union and the States (Articles 245-263)
Chapter I : Legislative Relations (Distribution of Law-Making Powers)

rows: Defines the territorial limits (Parliament for all of India; State Legislature for its State).
Distributes legislative competence according to the Seventh Schedule (Union, State, and Concurrent Lists).
Special provision with respect to GST
Grants concurrent power to both Union and States to make laws on GST; Parliament has exclusive power over IGST (Inter-State supply).
Exclusive power to legislate on any subject not listed is vested in the Parliament.
Power of Parliament to legislate on a State List subject in the National Interest.
Requires a Rajya Sabha resolution passed by a 2/3rd majority.
Power of Parliament to legislate on State List subjects during a National Emergency.
This power lasts only while the Proclamation of Emergency is in force and for six months thereafter.
Power of Parliament to legislate for two or more States by consent.
The States request Parliament to pass a law on a State List matter; the law applies only to those states.
Legislation for giving effect to International Agreements.
Empowers Parliament to make laws on any matter to implement international treaties, conventions, or agreements.
Inconsistency between laws made by Parliament and State Legislatures.
Central law prevails over a State law on the Concurrent List, with a saving clause if the State law received President's assent.
Requirements as to recommendations and previous sanctions
Clarifies that technical omissions regarding required recommendations (e.g., from the President) do not invalidate an Act if assent is given later.
State executive power must ensure compliance with laws made by Parliament; Centre can issue directions.
Control of the Union over States
States must not impede the executive power of the Union (e.g., maintenance of means of communication).
Union to confer powers on States
The President can entrust Union executive functions to a State government, with or without consent.
States to entrust functions to the Union
The Governor can entrust State executive functions to the Union government, with the Centre's consent.
Public acts, records and judicial proceedings
Requires "Full Faith and Credit" to be given to public acts and records across India (mutual recognition).
Adjudication of disputes relating to inter-State rivers.
Parliament can provide for the adjudication of such disputes and can bar the Supreme Court's jurisdiction.
Provisions with respect to an Inter-State Council (ISC).
Empowers the President to establish the ISC for better coordination and advice on common interests.

blocks.text: Four infographic panels at the foot of the page. Panel 1 — CONSTITUTIONAL BASIS OF GST: 101st Constitutional Amendment Act, 2016 → Article 246A: Concurrent Powers (Union & States); → Article 269A: Inter-State Trade (IGST Apportionment); → Article 279A: Establishes GST Council.

Panel 2 — GST Council: Constitutional Body: Union Finance Minister (Chair); Union Minister of State (Finance); Finance Ministers (All States). Union Vote: 1/3rd Weight | States' Collective Vote: 2/3nd Weight. Decisions: 75% Majority Required. RECOMMENDS: Tax Rates, Exemptions, Model Laws, Threshold Limits, Dispute Resolution.
Panel 3 — NATURE & TYPES OF GST: Excise, VAT, Service Tax, etc. → (funnel) → One Nation, One Tax (GST) → CGST (Intra-State), SGST (Intra-State), IGST (Inter-State).
